unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create a polished multi-section English product for a real audience, present it clearly, respond to questions and reflect on your Grade 5 growth.
unit.learningPath: Choose evidence. Review language. Plan and draft sections. Improve from feedback. Present. Reflect. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 5 learner presenting an independent English portfolio

readings.title: Review text 1: The Silence After the Rumble, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Ibrahim laughed. “Maybe. But I like being early. I like unlocking the doors. It feels like opening a promise.”
That evening, she walked home past burnt fences and empty plots where shops once stood. Her shoes crunched the gravel. From far off, she heard a soft rumble — thunder this time. The sky was grey and misty.
At home, her grandmother stirred soup over a fire. “The road may be cracked,” she said, “but we walk it anyway.”
“Yes,” Ibrahim said. “I walked it again today.”
They ate slowly. She saw her reflection in the metal spoon — dark eyes, tired but full of something new. Confidence maybe.
Later, she helped hang clean clothes behind their home. They flapped like flags in the cool air.
Suddenly, she encountered a boy her age. He had come to see her grandmother about a land document. “Do you work at the court?” he asked.
“Not really,” she said. Then she paused. “Actually, I do. I unlock the doors.”
The boy looked at her like she was important.
And maybe, she finally believed it too.

readings.title: Review text 2: The Burrow Discovery, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"Okay, okay," Selma said. "But I want snacks first."
The next day, they were walking to the community hall when they saw students outside, holding signs.
"They're starting a boycott against plastic cups in the lunchroom," someone said. "They want to protect the environment."
"I don't know," said Selma. "I'd rather research it first."
"We should support it," Noah said. "But let's find out more."
So they talked to the group leader. He showed them the data -- hundreds of cups used weekly, none recycled.
"You've got our approval," said Noah. "We'll help announce it next week."
That afternoon, they worked on their new blog:
"Today, we joined a group of students to start a boycott of plastic cups. We cooperated, shared ideas, and wrote a plan. Not everyone agrees yet, but we're working on it."
"We also learned: not everything in science goes fast. You must be patient. But when you mix loyalty, effort, and curiosity -- the results can be brilliant."
Now that you have read The Burrow Discovery, take some time to reflect on the story. Answer the questions below in full sentences.

readings.title: Review text 3: The Cracked Charter, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Nadia added:
“We will value all traditions — but not let them block progress.”
Noah closed with:
“Our system must be fairer, clearer, and more competitive, not in conflict — but in courage.”
They titled it: “The Students’ Charter of Hope.”
On the final day, the mayor returned. The students stood straight, their posture showing pride.
“You’ve written a beautiful document,” she said. “One day, some of you may serve in our legislative halls.”
“And I’ll build them,” said Nadia. “I want to be an architect.”
“And I’ll protect them,” said Adam. “Like a vulture guarding its hill.”
“You function well as a team,” the mayor said. “And that’s the most powerful kind of constitution.”
As they left, the cracked charter remained in its case — but now, it had new meaning. It was no longer just a broken page. It was a lesson.
A reminder that even fractures can lead to better foundations.

readings.title: Review text 4: The Hasty Route, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“Route A was more comfortable, less dangerous, and full of ancient clues. Route B was shorter but hotter and riskier. The dreadful wind made it miserable.”
“I was too forceful in my choice. Hannah’s slower plan was wiser. Her idea to explore carefully led to a profitable find.”
Later, around the fire, they sipped warm tea. Musa asked, “Do you think we’ll get chosen to present at the regional fair?”
“I think we’ve got a good chance,” Hannah replied. “Our site is unique, and we worked well together.”
Isaac nodded. “We disagreed, but we kept moving. That’s what made it honorable.”
The next day, the team began preparing their presentation.
“We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4,” said Hannah.
“And the decisions that taught them patience,” Isaac added.
They printed photos, diagrams, and their annotated map. The report included a defining clause:
“The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before.”
At the school fair, judges asked, “Was the storm a mistake?”
“No,” Hannah replied. “It was part of the journey.”
“And would you do it again?” they asked.
“Only with this team,” she smiled. “They’re the most faithful, most honorable crew imaginable.”
The story uses all 25 vocabulary words from Unit 4. Here are the ones to pay special attention to:
Find these examples in the story:
"The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before."
"...the explorers who found Desert Point 4..."
"...the decisions that taught them patience..."
The characters use the present continuous to talk about things already arranged:
"We’re documenting the full route tomorrow."
"We’re presenting the full trail report next week."
"We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4."

readings.title: Review text 5: The Memory Wall, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Adam raised his hand. “It’s mine.”
“Did you know that generations ago, stories were passed only by memory? You’ve honored that tradition.”
Adam felt his heart race — but it was not fear. It was pride.
At home that night, Adam’s little brother asked, “What’s a slave?”
Adam thought carefully. “A slave is someone who was forced to work without freedom. But they still had thoughts. Stories. Hope.”
“Yes,” Adam said, smiling. “And now I wrote it down so it won’t be forgotten.”
The next week, the Memory Wall was taken down — but not thrown away. Instead, it was turned into a book.
“Your work will be shared with next year’s students,” said Miss Ibrahim. “You helped build something real.”
“I didn’t expect that,” said Sarah.
“You believed in each other,” said Miss Ibrahim. “You united, didn’t you?”
“Definitely,” said Adam. “We didn’t exaggerate. We just told the truth.”

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

speaking.title: Portfolio choice conference
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain three portfolio choices and support each choice with a specific example.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

speaking.title: Fluent reading rehearsal
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Read a selected passage with clear phrasing, pace and expression.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

speaking.title: Vocabulary in context
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Use ten review words naturally while explaining your project.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

speaking.title: Three-minute presentation
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Present a clear opening, organised main points with evidence, and a strong closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

speaking.title: Question-and-answer practice
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Answer six audience questions in complete, relevant sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

speaking.title: Final capstone presentation
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Deliver your polished presentation and record your reflection.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my rehearsal and give feedback on organisation, vocabulary, fluency and audience awareness.

writing.title: Portfolio captions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of three selected pieces and explain what each shows.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organise facts and support an explanation with evidence.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Project proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your topic, audience, multi-section product and plan in two organised paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My project will teach younger learners about caring for nature. I will create an illustrated guide using facts and examples.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: First draft
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write the complete first draft of your capstone product with clear sections.
writing.modelText: Use a clear opening, organised sections and paragraphs, evidence or examples, and a conclusion suited to your audience.
writing.sentenceStarter: I want my audience to understand...
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Revision from feedback
writing.promptAndInstructions: Record two feedback points and rewrite the sections they improve.
writing.modelText: Feedback asked for stronger evidence, so I added a detail from Unit 7 and explained why it matters.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Presentation notes
writing.promptAndInstructions: Prepare concise cue cards for a three-minute presentation.
writing.modelText: Opening; three main ideas; evidence; audience question; closing.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Grade 5 reflection
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your strongest progress, one challenge, and a specific Grade 6 goal.
writing.modelText: I became a stronger reader because I learned to compare evidence. Next, I will improve paragraph transitions.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

activities.title: Curate three pieces
activities.instructionsAndItems: Review Units 1-9 and select reading, writing and speaking evidence.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

activities.title: Build a review word map
activities.instructionsAndItems: Organise 30 review words into meaningful categories and explain links.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

activities.title: Design the final product
activities.instructionsAndItems: Create a poster, booklet, report, story or digital presentation for a real audience.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

activities.title: Peer feedback studio
activities.instructionsAndItems: Use the rubric to give and receive two specific improvement suggestions.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

activities.title: Rehearse with timing
activities.instructionsAndItems: Practise the presentation twice and improve pace, volume and transitions.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

activities.title: Exhibition and celebration
activities.instructionsAndItems: Present the final product, answer questions and record your next learning goal.
activities.answerSummary: Teacher verifies completion against the capstone rubric and evidence checklist.

assignments.title: Grade 5 capstone portfolio and presentation
assignments.instructions: Submit the final multi-section product, three portfolio pieces, presentation recording and reflection.

quizzes.question: What does 'coal' mean?
quizzes.options: A black rock burned for heat and energy | Areas or parts of a country with something in common | To meet or come across someone by chance | When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
quizzes.correctAnswer: A black rock burned for heat and energy
quizzes.explanation: We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.

quizzes.question: What does 'pouch' mean?
quizzes.options: A feeling of worry or nervousness | A small bag or pocket that can hold things | Areas or parts of a country with something in common | When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
quizzes.correctAnswer: A small bag or pocket that can hold things
quizzes.explanation: He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.

quizzes.question: What does 'independence' mean?
quizzes.options: A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government | A very bright light that is hard to see in | The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices | To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
quizzes.correctAnswer: The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices
quizzes.explanation: On her birthday, she felt proud of her independence because she could choose her own cake.

quizzes.question: What does 'issue' mean?
quizzes.options: A place or thing where something comes from | A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about | A very bright light that is hard to see in | To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
quizzes.correctAnswer: A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about
quizzes.explanation: We had a big issue with our school project, so we worked together to fix it.

quizzes.question: What does 'policy' mean?
quizzes.options: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do. | A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works. | A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. | Related to making laws or rules.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do.
quizzes.explanation: Our school has a policy that everyone must wear a uniform.

quizzes.question: What does 'lobby' mean?
quizzes.options: A large area inside a building where people wait or meet. | A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. | A special job or task that someone is trying to do. | Related to making laws or rules.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A large area inside a building where people wait or meet.
quizzes.explanation: We waited in the lobby of the museum for our guide.

quizzes.question: What does 'hasty' mean?
quizzes.options: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully. | Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Very angry or mad. | Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully.
quizzes.explanation: He made a hasty decision to leave the game early.

quizzes.question: What does 'reluctant' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily annoyed or made angry. | Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Not wanting to do something or feeling unsure about it. | Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not wanting to do something or feeling unsure about it.
quizzes.explanation: She was reluctant to try the new food because she didn't know if she would like it.

quizzes.question: What does 'communicate' mean?
quizzes.options: To convince someone to do or believe something. | To make someone believe that something is true. | To make something seem important by saying it clearly or strongly. | To share information or ideas with someone.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To share information or ideas with someone.
quizzes.explanation: Good writers communicate their ideas clearly.

quizzes.question: What does 'quote' mean?
quizzes.options: A group of words that someone has said or written. | To convince someone to do or believe something. | To make someone believe that something is true. | To support someone or give them confidence.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A group of words that someone has said or written.
quizzes.explanation: Sarah shared a quote from her grandmother.
